item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
angular momentum, and refractive index consistent with glass of the same dimensions

This means something along the lines of, "spins as fast as glass," which is as nonsense as it sounds. Is there a different definition of angular momentum related to the ability to polarize light, which would make sense? Sorry if that's the case, but you should delete angular momentum otherwise. I took a look at the Wikipedia article about spin angular momentum of light, but that doesn't seem to be a material property either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So I'm going to have to agree with some of the above posts. This is a fun and interesting idea but it lacks a lot of core information about the scip. Ultimately the Foundation is about containment and in this case that means the cylinder (of whatever dimensions beyond being a cylinder as that is never specified). The alternate dimension may be of interest to Foundation researchers but the cylinder is what they must focus on. The mention of a test subject/researcher dying of radiation poisoning strongly suggests bleed through of a dangerous nature. This again needs to be considered in the containment description (perhaps the scip could be stored in a lead lined tube). Overall I have to give this a very reluctant -1 with the hope that some day the containment and description can be fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this but I feel like the twist (they ended their own world because of the end of the Mayan calendar) could have been better.

What if instead of them causing it, their world actually did end when the Mayan calendar ended. Like, the twist is that it does show the end of the world. Just not our world.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I, too, was disappointed that an alternate universe where the Chinese discovered the Americas wasn't more fleshed out, because that could be way, way cool, but I fucking loved that ending. It caught me totally off guard, even after I had the date all up in my face.

But I was thinking, why would they need us to be obsessed with the world ending in 2012? Wouldn't it already maybe be a big part of the Mayan culture?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You earned my first downvote! Grats!

+ Long explanation here.
+ Hide text wall.

Okay, leaving aside the whole "you guys suck at doomsday" thing, which has some potential -maybe-, this isn't a scientific article at all: there's just too much info missing (and not in a [REDACTED] way).
How big is it? How heavy is it? Can you get samples from it, and, if you can, what results have said samples yielded? Does this thing regenerate damage? On the other hand, can it be damaged at all? I'm not saying "exploding in a shattercloud of irreparable damage" damaged, but "scratched like normal glass by diamond, iron, whatever" damaged.
How can you see the alternate world, that is to say: can you see it "through" the cylinder as if you were just staring through a normal glass cylinder, can you see a 3D 'hologram' inside of it…? This is kind of a minor issue (I guess the reader could assume it), yet it's supposed to be stated.
There's also some major plotholes. If the cylinder is stuck in a "tube", and the world where the "tube" is in is completely and utterly frozen, how did anyone from that frozen world remove the tube? If that world is frozen -but besides that, if it's a whole another world (thus supposedly unreachable from us), how the hell can you read that world's documentation? Do you just so happen to conveniently find some redacted files and history books open at the right pages, and in the right places? Also, if that world is frozen, how the hell do light and radiations come through it? And no, I won't let it go.
Elaborating on the documentation, how the bloody hell did the Foundation get to know so much about that world's storyline by simply carrying around a bloody inter-dimensional looking glass? Again, I'm not buying that they just so happened to have found some info just so conveniently laying around… especially in so convenient places that they don't even have to intrude any building in any current-world city, which is what I got from reading this. Speaking of which, the Foundation just goes around carrying a [REDACTED]-sized bloody inter-dimensional window in Manhattan and Paris? Seriously? But I guess that's minor, the Foundation could've found some ways to use it without having any civilian witness it. Still, I believe said ways should be stated.
Oh, also, I get it that otherwordly obstacles are physical to this looking glass, right? Didn't Foundation agents have to climb up (or down, for that matter) otherworldly ramps and/or stairs, at least in the pyramid-powerplants? And in the otherworldly Site 19, as well, since it's built both over- and underground, although that Site is a replica and, thus, has supposedly got the same architecture as the Site where the cylinder is housed in. Still, if they in some place had to, how did they get through it? Did they burrow into the ground and built stairs to match the otherworldly ones? Again, I would've stated that.

Yeah, I probably could've summed all of this up in a better way. I also agree with Goodwill, first page of the comments.

+ Hide text wall.
+ tl;dr
+ The tl;dr was tl;dr

Overall, the idea could have some potential, but it needs to be polished into a precise, scientific, Foundation-like article.


Also, I apologize if someone else already said all of this stuff, I didn't read all the comments.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with those who commented before, I've got no clue of this object's dimensions because you didn't specify any. And the "we must make the end of the world happen!" thing is nice, but as of now, it seems hamfisted and tongue-in-cheek. Maybe if you made it a little more subtle…(Or, you know, eliminated the addendum completely and let people reach their own conclusions!)

Downvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The only thing keeping me from throwing my +1 at this is the uncertain nature of the object itself.

The foundation has a massive hard-on for protocol, measurements, and empirical data.

basic size, method of use, clearance lv for experimentation, etc.

there doesn't seem to be enough of a description or analysis of the physical skip itself for an SCP object.

withheld for nao.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with this assessment. The descriptions of the significant locations give just enough information to allow the reader to imagine their own reasons for the alternate-Mayan turning on their world.

If the note must remain, I would suggest altering the motive to give the alternate-Mayans more agency in their decision and adding a stronger element of cultural cross-contamination. Otherwise, it's a great entry in the alternate timeline field.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was suckered in too much by the WTFuttery of it all to let the dumb end note ruin it all for me. In my opinion, removing that note entirely makes this that much more mysterious and powerful

Everything about it sets up perfectly to that, and the end note telling us it directly is unnecessary for an otherwise awesomet hing

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you, I'll do that. I'll humbly admit that I simply used Google Translate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sending him one as we speak. Directing him to look at my post here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Have you tried contacting the author about this? If they don't respond, send me a PM and I'll talk to staff about implementing the change.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My gripe is with the bit of Chinese in the recovered document. I'm a Chinese myself, so let me explain a bit:

"美国帝国" can mean "the America Empire" alright, but "美国" itself literally means "the State (or Country) of America". "美国帝国" would more literally mean "the State of America Empire", which makes little sense.

"美国" is a compound word and a contraction; "美" literally means "beautiful", but in this case it's a shortening of the archaic transliteration of the word "America" in Chinese: "阿美利加" (ā měi lì jīa in Pinyin, if I remember correctly), while 国 means state or country. Since it is implied that in this alternate timeline, America never became a country in itself but instead became an empire with China, in Chinese it could be referred instead as "阿美利加帝国" (literally, America Empire or Empire of America), using the archaic term for America. Quite possibly, the term "美国", which is shortened from "阿美利加国" (meaning State of America, but with the archaic term used here), would have never arose in this alternate timeline. Of course, there is a possibility they could have shortened it to "美帝国", but it sounds awkward in Chinese Mandarin and gives rise to a few unfortunate puns with unfortunate meanings (remember, every Chinese character has an inherent meaning).

My suggestion is to change "美国帝国" to "阿美利加帝国" for the document to make more sense. Otherwise, this SCP tells quite an intriguing story linked to our superstitious beliefs surrounding December 21st 2012. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just curious, since it appears to be frozen in time, did they actually employ some sort of doomsday thing which actually stopped time, which was somehow related to the pyramids? That would explain how the guy got radiation poisoning from a world in which time has stopped. It feels unfinished. A log which explained more about their culture, the tech behind the pyramids, and exactly what they did to end the world and just why it's frozen would add to it enough for me to upvote.

(Didn't mean to reply to Chris, sorry)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not bad. A few confusing point was made clear while reading this discussion page
However I'm surprised no one noted how the writer missed a basic description of this object:
for example, the height, width, radius and etc.
For this reason I found it hard to imagine how it would be like using this object:
Are they using it like kaleidoscope, or maybe it stands tall and wide and researchers are surrounding it, looking at its inside?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: … wait, how big is it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This has definitely been posted before… it seems very very familiar and yet it was posted after I began my hiatus from this site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There needs to be a bit more in the description about the mechanics of the co-location. It's clear from the initial discovery and exploration logs that it can't be moved through solid objects on the other side, but stating that behavior explicity would both make it clearer to the reader and be better scientific tone.

Excellent alterna-history; and while I don't mind the "we can DO that!" end of the world scenario, it would be improved if you focused more on the alien society that coudl produce such thinking. The actual boom is nowhere near as creepy as the people who would en masse work to make it happen.

Overall, a good read, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh duh, I should've known that it changes, logically the longest day of the year won't always fall on the same calendar date. Thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The solstice is an actual event, and the date changes sometimes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This may be a nitpick, but wouldn't "December 21st" be more in-tone than "Winter Solstice"? Otherwise, I sort of like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
yeah, you had me up until the end. I'm not sure why you'd take a alternate history with cultural exchange and contact between China and the Americas and then use it for a lame 2012 joke.
No vote, because I'd like to see the alternate history stuff stay around in a different form

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But that's exactly what appears to have happened :-

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yep. This feels like Insane Troll Logic on a civillization-wide scale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe make it a single organization that got some insane SCP that ends the world. Maybe the Foundation itself goes cuckoo for coco puffs. I dunno.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Has this been posted or drafted before? It looks familiar.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The justification for this alternate world destroying itself is kinda weak, and I have to agree that it's a bit of a letdown to be led into a world where Zheng He actually traveled to the Americas just to have it end with a Mayan Calendar tie-in. Also, maybe I'm just having a bit of a brain fart right now, but the description seems kinda vague. How is the Foundation exploring this? Is it like a portal or a window? No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I get it if the hook doesn't do much for you, but it's not like the entire alternate universe needs to be in on it. Just the right people who have the willingness and ability to make people do it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like alternate universes and such and this one is pretty decent, so I upvoted.

That said, it kinda feels unfinished somehow, like there needs to be more of it. But that might just be my desire for more exploration logs god I love me some exploration logs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Look, all they're saying is that we need to be a bit more proactive when it comes to the end of the world. We can't just sit around, waiting for it to happen. We've got to step up and get it done!

Also, I hoped I'd made it clear enough in the addendum that not everyone was in on the whole 2012 shtick, just the people who wrote the helpful note. Heck, even their Chinese allies of 500 years weren't too happy about it when bad stuff started happening.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this. It took me a couple of rereads to figure out that the object shows you where you're at, before I got to the exploration log. It's set up well, presents an interesting world, and reads pretty clearly, my hamfisted confusion aside.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am also disappointed that it doesn't have a payout beyond "lol world ends in 2012".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not sure what's going on here. So the alternate universe was like "wow, you're obsessed with the end of the world, and that matters for some reason, so now we're going to preemptively end ourselves"? How did an entire world civilization arrive at that conclusion? Nobody stopped to think at any point "hey, why exactly are we feeding ourselves into giant scary pyramids"? Were their leaders C'Tan?

Sorry, not buying it. -1

As for improving this, I can't speak to everyone else's preferences, but you've got an alternate, Chinese-influenced world order that could be interesting if it was developed better. Instead it gets shunted off into "lol 2012". It's kind of a letdown.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh, this was decently interesting. I was entertained. +1

Perhaps the Chinese name at the end should be translated in a footnote?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd love to know if there's anything I can change to get it beyond "barely good enough". Having put up versions of this months ago in the sandbox with criticism of varying degrees of usefulness, any input now would be welcome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree, minus the "just woke up" part. It's just barely good enough to upvote, but I feel like one small error that I glanced over would change my mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe I'm in a good mood because I just woke up but I kind of like this more than I dislike it. Not by very much, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So there's that. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not just our modern interpretation of the Mayan calendar but how we view the Millennium and everything else. The people who happen to be watching us have concluded that our fascination on the end of the world based on an arbitrary calendar date must have some sort of significance. Unfortunately for them, they took the wrong lesson out of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, the people on that side thought that, because some of the people on our end interpreted the Mayan calendar as countdown to the end, they should make their world end on 2012?

Those guys are morons. Or I am.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's right, it's a cylindrical object! I'm totally a rebel!
